premise: The signature in-group/out-group instrument and the 5th/last cast NPC: a newcomer (new neighbour) whose circle-membership is deliberately unsettled and EVOLVES. Each low-stakes encounter re-poses widen-the-circle (resolution:circle-widened) vs hold-it (resolution:circle-held) with her less of a stranger each time, paired with an abstract twin (a generic newcomer). Two signals: the per-beat debiasing gap (a specific person vs a stranger-in-principle) AND the TRAJECTORY of inclusion across beats — does your circle widen as she becomes known? Scored on the circle-widened/held inclusion poles (universalism<->particularism), NOT the loyalty axis. Descriptive, no aggregate score.

beats.title: The first week
beats.intro: Marisol moved into 2B over the weekend. It's a few days later now — a handful of hellos on the stairs and not much else. She's the new one, still working out which bins are which, and you don't owe her anything yet.
beats.setup:
Marisol moved in this week. You know the shape of being new because you've been new here too: the first days where you nod at everyone in the hall and belong to nothing, where you carry your coffee down to the little shared courtyard a beat too early or too late because you can't read the rhythm of the place yet.

There's a rhythm here. Most fair evenings a few of you drift down to the bench and the step by the back door — no announcement, no invite, it just happens, the same handful of flats who've lived stacked on top of each other long enough that the talk has its own shorthand. It's a small, worn-in thing, half the reason the building feels like anything at all, and it's one of the parts of the day you actually like.

It's a mild evening now. You're heading down with your mug when you clock Marisol on the stairs, keys still in her hand, doing the thing where you linger over the postboxes so it isn't obvious you've nowhere to be. She's friendly enough — wry, quick, a little careful, the way people are when they're new. She hasn't asked you for anything. She wouldn't. She's just there at the edge of it all with nowhere in particular to go, and you're the only one who's noticed.

scenes.text: Voices carry up from the courtyard — the bench is filling for the evening. There's room down there; there's always room, nobody counts. You could carry your mug down and let the usual hour happen the way it always does. Or you could do something small first. Marisol catches your eye for half a second before she goes back to the postboxes. Nothing is owed here; she's days in and a stranger, and any version of this is fine. You:

choices.text: Catch her on the stairs. "A few of us sit out the back most evenings — come down, if you want." Make a place at the bench before she has to wonder whether there is one.
Stop a moment on your way past. "Heads up — half the building ends up on the back step around now, in case you're ever after company." Tell her how it works, then go take your usual spot.
Give her a warm nod on the way down and let her find the rhythm herself, the way you eventually did. Head out to the bench.

scenes.text: Something in her shoulders drops. "Yeah? If it's not weird." It isn't; she comes down with her own mug and the bench shuffles up without anyone making a thing of it. It's a little stiffer than usual at first — an old joke needs explaining and goes flat once it's explained — but by the time it's dark she's laughing at something, properly, for the first time since she arrived. She doesn't come every evening after that, but she comes, and within a week or two nobody clocks her as the new one anymore. The worn-in bench is a fraction looser at the edges than it was, and you find you don't mind. You spent ten seconds and a small bit of the thing you liked exactly as it was, and a stranger got somewhere to sit. As trades go, it barely registered. You'd make it again.

She takes it the way it's given — "good to know," a friendly nod — and files it away, grateful for the map even if she doesn't use it tonight. You head down to the bench and the usual hour happens, easy and worn-in, the same as always. She stays up in 2B. Once, mid-sentence, you catch the light come on in her window above you, new, alone; the thought isn't sharp, it's just there, and then the evening carries on. You kept your circle the size it works best at, and nobody would think to fault you for it — she's new, and new is something everyone has to walk through. You watch her start to walk through it. Some of that's on its own clock now, not yours.

beats.reflection_prompt: When you noticed Marisol with nowhere to go, you chose to {chosen_path}. She's still mostly a stranger — so what were you weighing: what a new neighbour needed from someone, or the easy worn-in thing you didn't feel like changing for someone you barely know yet?

abstract_twin.prompt: Someone brand new turns up in your orbit this week — a new neighbour, a new face, a stranger a few days in. They haven't asked you for anything, and there's a small, easy circle you already belong to that they're plainly outside of. You:

options.text: Pull them in a little — make room, offer them a place at the thing you've already got going.
Stay friendly but separate — be cordial, and let them find their own footing the way everyone does.

beats.title: A spot, a chat, a key
beats.intro: It's been a few weeks of small collisions in the stairwell, and Marisol isn't a stranger now — she's the name you have for the woman in 2B, the one who waters the plant in the hall nobody else remembered. Tonight the building's one good thing, and the quiet web of favours that runs it, is sitting in your hands.
beats.setup:
Your building has exactly one parking space worth having — the wide one off the lane, out of the wind, the only one a delivery van can't box you into — and an unspoken arrangement, older than you, about who gets it. There's a list in nobody's handwriting: the four or five flats who've been here long enough to know it exists, who text the group chat when they're away so somebody else takes the spot, who hold a parcel, who have each other's spare keys in a kitchen drawer for the day someone's locked out. It isn't a rule. It's just the people you actually know, looking out for the people you actually know.

Marisol moved into 2B in the spring. At first she was the new one — a nod on the stairs, the cordial nothing you give anyone. But it's been enough weeks now that the nod has a name on it. She's the one who chased your blown-off recycling lid down the lane in the rain. She brought the wrong-address parcel up two flights rather than leave it for the post to take back. You know she works odd hours, that she's polite to the point of apology, that she has nobody else in the building yet — she's still on the outside of the thing the rest of you don't even think of as a thing.

Tonight you're heading off for three days. You'd normally text the chat, leave the good spot for whoever's around, drop your spare with the flat across the landing. And as you're carrying your bag down, Marisol's coming up, and she stops, and — a little embarrassed to be asking anyone for anything — says her car's been getting clipped out front, twice now, and is there, she's heard there's a better spot somewhere, she doesn't want to step on anybody.

scenes.text: She says it lightly, already half-apologising, leaving you every easy out — "only if it's free, I really don't mind." The good spot is free for three days. You've got the spare key in your jacket and the group chat open on your phone, the small machinery of the building right there in your hands. You can fold her into it, or you can keep it the way it's always run.

choices.text: Bring her in properly. "Take the good spot while I'm gone — off the lane, nobody clips you there." Get her number, add her to the chat, and press your spare into her hand: "And here. So there's two of us who can let you in. Welcome to the racket."

Lend her the spot, this once, and leave the rest where it is. "Course — take mine the next few days, it's just sitting empty." The chat and the keys are the regulars' thing; she can earn into that the way everyone did, in time. "We'll sort you out properly when I'm back."

Point her to it warmly, but keep the arrangement as it is. "There's a good one off the lane — grab it whenever it's open, honestly." The spare and the chat stay with the people who've been here for years; it's not yours alone to hand out. "You'll get the lay of it."

scenes.text: She laughs, surprised, and takes the key like it weighs something. "Are you sure? You don't even — okay. Okay, thank you." By the time you're back the chat has three new messages with her name on them — she took a parcel for the flat upstairs, moved her car so the van could turn, asked if anyone needed anything from the late shop. She's not the new one anymore; she's one of the ones who looks out, because somebody let her in close enough to. It cost you a spare key and a slightly fuller chat. You find you don't think of it as having cost you anything.

"You're a lifesaver," she says, and means it, and her car sits safe off the lane the whole time you're gone. When you're back she's left the spot exactly as she found it and a thank-you note under your door, neat and a little formal. The good turn was real and she's warmer to you for it. She's still outside the rest of it, though — the chat, the keys, the quiet web — the way you were once outside it too, before enough time made you part of the furniture. Maybe that comes for her. Tonight you did the one kind thing and kept the circle the size it's always been, which is a defensible size for a circle to be.

beats.reflection_prompt: When Marisol asked, half-apologising, for a way in, you chose to {chosen_path}. Looking back — was that about how well you actually knew her yet, or about how much of your own small comfort it would have cost to bring her all the way in?

abstract_twin.prompt: Someone fairly new to your building — you've seen them around, you'd recognise them, but you don't really know them — needs a small thing your established little group quietly handles among itself (a shared resource, a turn in a rotation, the informal favour-swapping the regulars do). It would cost you almost nothing to fold them in. You:

options.text: Bring them in — add them to the group, share the resource, treat them as one of you.
Help them out this once, but keep the group as it is — they can become a regular over time the way everyone does.

beats.title: The open seat
beats.intro: It's been the better part of a year now. Marisol's not the new neighbour anymore — she's just Marisol: in the building chat, on the back step without anyone arranging it, the one who knows where you keep the spare chairs. Tonight there's one open spot on a plan, and it's yours to fill.
beats.setup:
Every year the old core of the building — the four flats who go back years, who had each other's keys before you ever arrived — rents the same lake cabin for a long weekend in September. It sleeps five. For as long as it's been a thing, the fifth bed has floated: someone's partner one year, someone's brother another, sometimes just left empty and nice to have spare. This year the booking's done and the fifth spot is open, and it's landed on you to sort, the way these small logistics do.

The obvious name is Marisol. Somewhere over these months she stopped being the newcomer in 2B and became just one of the regulars — she's in the chat, she's out on the step on a fair evening, she fed your plants in April and didn't make it a favour. She'd love the cabin. She'd fit. You already know she's free that weekend because she said so, lightly, last week, the way you mention free weekends to people you assume might fill them.

And yet the cabin is the old thing — the four flats, the specific worn-in shorthand of people who were stacked in this building before any of this. Marisol's a regular now, genuinely. Whether she's a regular or one of the four is a line you've never actually had to draw out loud. Tonight, filling the bed, you draw it.

scenes.text: The booking confirmation is open on your phone, the building chat under it. One of the four has just typed: "who's got the 5th bed this yr?" — and then, a beat later, "marisol??", with the question marks that mean they've thought of her too and are leaving it to you. The easiest thing is to type her name in like it was never a question. The other easy thing is to say you'll take the spare or leave it open — keep the cabin the four-flats thing it's always been, no harm, no announcement. You:

choices.text: Type it without ceremony: "yeah obvi marisol — i'll ask her." Then ask her, like the answer was always going to be yes. (count her in by default — she's one of yours now)
"i'll sort it" — and quietly keep the fifth bed for the old four, spare or empty. Not against Marisol; just that the cabin stays the thing it's been. (hold the line — regular, not one of the four)
Ask her, but soft-pedal it so a no is easy: "long shot, the cabin's kind of an old-building thing, but the 5th bed's open if you ever fancied it." (open the door, but hold it half-closed)

scenes.text: She reads it, you can tell — the typing dots come and go. "ah that's so nice of you," she sends, and then, reading the room exactly: "honestly don't worry, that sounds like a you-four thing — next time tho!" She means it warmly; she's giving you the out you half-offered. The door's still ajar. You can let her hand it back, or push it the rest of the way open.

choices.text: Push it open: "it's not though, not anymore — you're one of us, come. i'll put you down." (take the out away; count her in for real)
Let her have it: "hah yeah — next time for sure 🙂" The cabin stays the four; she stays the neighbour you're glad to have the rest of the year.

scenes.text: She says yes the way you knew she would — a little surprised under the easy yes, the surprise of someone clocking that they've been counted in without having to earn it this time. In September there are five of you at the lake, and the old shorthand bends to make room and doesn't break; by Sunday there's a new in-joke that's hers. The four-flats thing is a five-of-you thing now, quietly, with no announcement. You drew the line tonight and you drew it around her. It cost you nothing you can name, and it changed, a little, what the thing is.

"...okay, yeah," she sends, and then a second message that's just an exclamation, the unguarded kind. You put her down for the fifth bed. It took you the length of one soft-pedalled text to decide she was in, but you got there: at the lake she's one of the five, and the worn-in thing makes room. You notice you nearly left the door half-shut. You notice you're glad you opened it the rest of the way.

You take the spare bed yourself and let the fifth spot be the old four. In the chat it reads as nothing — logistics handled. Marisol never knows there was a seat, which is the point; nothing was refused, because nothing was offered out loud. The cabin in September is the same four it's always been, the same easy unguarded shorthand, and it's good the way it's reliably good. Some part of you is aware she'd have come, and would have fit, and that you kept the oldest thing the size it's always been. It isn't guilt. It's just a line you drew, and felt yourself draw.

She lets it go, lightly, no wound in it, and the rest of the year stays exactly as it's been — the chat, the back step, the neighbour who isn't a stranger anymore. The cabin stays the four. You gave her a real warm year and kept the one old thing whole, and she never seems to count it against you; it just becomes a shape the friendship has. Now and then, the September weekend, you think of her free that weekend and not at the lake. The thought isn't sharp. It's just there, the way a held line is there.

beats.reflection_prompt: When the open seat was yours to fill, you chose to {chosen_path}. After all these months — was that about what the old thing is worth keeping whole, or about who Marisol has actually become to you by now?

abstract_twin.prompt: Someone has been around the edges of your close group for a long while now — a year, more. Friendly, familiar, reliably there, but never formally one of the inner circle. A spot opens that's only ever gone to the core. You:

options.text: Count them in by default — at this point they're one of you; the inner circle should just include them now.
Keep the spot for the core. They're a valued regular, and that's a real place — but the inner circle stays the inner circle.
